entry.title: Re: Is PO Creation via RAP (EML) using I_PURCHASEORDERTP_...
entry.content:
Hello @S_DEV 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, you can create purchase orders from a custom RAP application in Public Cloud by using EML on the released BO interface I_PURCHASSEORDERTP_2, provided you execute the EML call inside the Public Cloud ABAP environment. This is what the internal ABAB Cloud pattern supports.

This similar question might be of good help: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/how-to-build-a-rap-application-to-upload-program-in-s-4-hana-cloud-public/qaq-p/13783675

However, there are two key restrictions you should be aware of, namely:

  1. This solution is not an external integration interface. External systems should not call EML directly. For external integration, use a released business service or the purchase order APIs instead.
  2. PO type support is limited. See KBA 3482522 for details on I_PURCHASSEORDERTP_2 via EML.

As a next step, I suggest you build the upload logic in your custom RAP app, test the EML create with PO type NB, validate your result in Manage Purchase Orders,  

and only use SAP APIs if your requirement is true external system integration. If your business requires non NB document types or broader purchasing scenarios, the approach will hit SAP standard limitations and you must re-evaluate it with the released APIs.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Step-by-Step Integration of SAP Cloud ALM with S/4HANA for Transport Management
entry.content:
This blog outlines the process of integrating SAP Cloud ALM with SAP S/4 HANA to enable efficient transport management. The integration ensures streamlined lifecycle management, improved visibility, and better control over transport activities across the SAP landscape.

The overall process involves the following key steps:

  1. Onboarding SAP Cloud ALM

Before initiating the integration, SAP Cloud ALM must be provisioned and onboarded in your SAP environment.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following requirement is met before starting the onboarding process:

You must have an S-user ID with the “Edit Cloud Data” authorization in SAP for Me.

Procedure

  • Log in to SAP for Me.
  • Navigate to Systems & Provisioning.
  • Under Products Available for Provisioning, locate your entitlement for SAP Cloud ALM.

  • Choose Start Provisioning.

Select the geographical region in which you would like to provision SAP Cloud ALM 

  • Enter a unique subdomain name.
  • Specify an SAP Cloud Identity Service, if you have an existing one please select it from the list, if you do not have an existing SAP Cloud Identity Service a new one will be created for you automatically.

  • After selecting Submit, the provisioning process is initiated. SAP Cloud ALM is automatically prepared by SAP, and the required foundational entities are created to enable the service.

During this process, the following components are provisioned:

  • A SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) Global Account and Subaccount dedicated to SAP Cloud ALM are created. Within this subaccount, you will generate the Service API key, which is required to connect SAP Cloud ALM with other systems in your landscape.
  • A mutual trust relationship is automatically established between SAP Cloud ALM and the selected SAP Cloud Identity Authentication Service tenant to enable secure authentication and user access.

Once SAP completes the setup of these entities, you will receive a confirmation email containing the URL to access your newly provisioned SAP Cloud ALM tenant.

  1. System Prerequisites

SAP_BASIS Release

Your ABAP system must meet one of the following requirements:

  • SAP_BASIS 7.40 SP16 or higher (or 7.50 SP05 or higher)
  • SAP_BASIS 7.40 SP09 – SP15 (or 7.50 SP00 – SP04) with implementation of SAP Note 2283880

SAP_UI Version

The minimum required version is:

  • SAP_UI 740 SP15 or higher

ST-PI Component

Ensure the ST-PI component is properly maintained:

  • Install ST-PI 7.40 support packages and keep them up to date.
  • Implement the required collective correction SAP Note 3639977, which includes updates for SAP Cloud ALM compatibility.
  • Always operate the ABAP system with the latest or second latest support package available on the SAP Support Portal.

ST-A/PI Version

For Business Process Monitoring in SAP S/4HANA or SAP Business Suite 7:

  • Use ST-A/PI version higher than 01U_731.
  • Keep the component updated regularly.
  • If using ST-A/PI version 01W, ensure that the latest related SAP Notes are implemented.

Security and SSL Configuration

The following profile parameters and certificates must be configured in the ABAP system:

Required Profile Parameters

  • icm/HTTPS/client_sni_enabled = TRUE
  • ssl/client_ciphersuites must be maintained according to SAP Note 510007

Required Root Certificates

The following certificates must be imported in STRUST under:

  • SSL Client (Anonymous)
  • SSL Client (Standard)

Certificates required:

  • DigiCert Global Root G2
  • DigiCert TLS RSA4096 Root G5

Required User Authorizations

The user who runs transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP must be assigned the following PFCG role:

  • SAP_SDF_ALM_SETUP

Within this role, maintain the authorization object:

  • S_BTCH_NAM → BTCUNAME

The value for BTCUNAME can be maintained as:

  • * (asterisk) to allow any user to execute the background job, or
  • The specific username that will run the background job for data collection.

Background Job User designated to run the data collection background jobs must have the appropriate roles and authorizations required for SAP Cloud ALM data integration. 

Configure Transport Management System (STMS) in SAP S/4HANA

Log in to the SAP S/4HANA application server and configure the SAP Transport Management System (STMS) to enable transport management across your system landscape.

In this example, a two-system landscape is used:

  • Development System: NI2
  • Production System: PI2

The first step is to establish the STMS configuration between the NI2 (Development) system and the PI2 (Production) system. This configuration ensures that transport requests created in the development system can be properly routed and imported into the production system following the defined transport path.

Make sure CTC parameter value maintained in DEV and prod system as 1

  1. Connecting the On-Premise System to SAP Cloud ALM

Once the SAP Transport Management System (STMS) configuration is completed between the systems, the next step is to integrate the on-premise SAP S/4HANA system with SAP Cloud ALM.

This integration enables SAP Cloud ALM to monitor transport activities, manage deployment processes, and provide centralized visibility across the entire system landscape. 

Integration Setup Across System Landscapes

To establish the integration with SAP Cloud ALM, the required configuration steps must be performed in both the Development (DEV) and Production (PROD) systems. In addition, the setup should be executed in client 000 as well as in the productive client of each system.

While the technical configuration steps remain largely the same across environments, the purpose and use cases of each client and system differ:

  • Client 000 – Typically used for system-level configuration and technical setup activities.
  • Productive Client – Used for business operations and application-related processes.
  • Development (DEV) System – Used for development, configuration, and testing activities.
  • Production (PROD) System – Used for live business operations.

By ensuring that the integration is properly configured across these systems and clients, SAP Cloud ALM can effectively monitor system activities, manage transports, and support lifecycle operations across the entire SAP landscape. 

Login to 000 in client /n/SDF/ALM_SETUP

Maintaining the HTTP Destination

To configure the HTTP destination, a service key from SAP Cloud ALM is required. This service key contains the necessary credentials and endpoint details that allow the on-premise system to securely communicate with the cloud environment.

The service key is generated within SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), where SAP Cloud ALM is provisioned.

Procedure

  1. Log in to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) cockpit.
  2. Navigate to the subaccount associated with your SAP Cloud ALM tenant.
  3. Locate the SAP Cloud ALM service instance.
  4. Generate a service key, which will be used to maintain the HTTP destination in the on-premise system.

The information from the service key will later be used to configure the required communication settings between the on-premise SAP S/4HANA system and SAP Cloud ALM.

Registering the System in SAP Cloud ALM

Enter the background user that was created to perform the data collection tasks. Ensure that this user has the required authorizations as described in the Prerequisites section.

Next, choose Register to initiate the connection with SAP Cloud ALM and register the system. If the registration is successful, an LMS ID from the Landscape Management Service is automatically retrieved and displayed. This ID confirms that the system has been successfully registered in SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. Activating Transport Management Use Cases in SAP Cloud ALM

After registering the system using transaction /SDF/ALM_SETUP, the next step is to activate the required transport-related use cases in SAP Cloud ALM.
The tasks to be activated depend on the system landscape (DEV, QUA, PRD) and the client in which the configuration is performed.

  1. Development System (DEV)

Client 000: Activate the following task:

Transports: Read; This task must be activated on source systems to report transport requests to SAP Cloud ALM so that they can be assigned to features.

Client 100 (Development Productive Client)

Activate: Transports: Create & Export (Client-Specific)

This task must be activated on all development clients used for customizing activities.

For example:

  • If customizing activities are performed in client 100, activate the task there.
  • If another client such as 200 is used for development or transport creation, the task must also be activated in that client.

Activating this task triggers the background job:

/SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_PROC_CL_DEP-XXX

This enables the following transport-related functions:

  • Create transports
  • Create transport of copies
  • Release transports
  • Delete empty transports during release

Transports: Retrofit (Client-Specific)

Activate this task in the development system of your maintenance track if retrofit functionality is required.

Retrofit ensures that changes implemented in one landscape can be synchronized with another development track.

  1. Quality System (QUA)

Client 000; Activate the following task:

Transports: Import

This triggers the background job:

/SDF/CALM_CDM_IMPORT_TRANSPORTS

The job performs the following actions:

  • Retrieves transports scheduled for import from SAP Cloud ALM
  • Executes the transport import process into the target system

Target systems typically include:

  • Quality Assurance (QA)
  • Pre-Production
  • Production

These systems are assigned to the project via the deployment plan.

  1. Production System (PRD)

Client 000; Activate the following tasks:

Transports: Import

Allows SAP Cloud ALM to trigger transport imports into the production system.

Transports: Read Landscape

This task reports the STMS landscape configuration to SAP Cloud ALM.

Supported landscape configurations include:

  • Any consistent Transport Management System (TMS) landscape
  • The last system in a transport track is always considered the production system
  • Client-specific transport routes (CTC) are recommended
  • TMS transport groups are supported

Transports: Check; This feature enables transport impact analysis before importing transports into production.

It evaluates the transport set to identify potential risks or conflicts that may impact the production environment.

Do the same for 000 and productive client of production system as well.

  1. Background Jobs Created After SAP Cloud ALM Configuration

Once the configuration and system registration are completed in SAP Cloud ALM, several background jobs are automatically scheduled in the SAP S/4HANA (or ABAP) system. These jobs are responsible for data collection, system monitoring, transport processing, and communication with SAP Cloud ALM.

Some of the commonly scheduled jobs include:

  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_DIAGNOSTICS_000
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_DIAGNOSTICS_<Productive Client>
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_CHECK
  • /SDF/CALM_CDM_TR_PROC_CL_DEP-<Client No.>

In addition, the following operational jobs may also be scheduled:

  • CALM Auto Discovery – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_00
  • CALM Heartbeat – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_000
  • CALM Scheduler – RFCALMPI1<Client No.> / ZCALM_PI1_000

These jobs ensure continuous communication, monitoring, and transport management synchronization between the on-premise system and SAP Cloud ALM.

Now go to projects and setup and add the system.

Add the systems to SAP Cloud ALM based on the number of systems available in your landscape. In this example, the environment consists of two landscapes: Development and Production. These systems are added and organized accordingly to enable proper monitoring, transport management, and lifecycle operations within SAP Cloud ALM.

  1. Creating Deployment Plans

The next step is to create deployment plans in SAP Cloud ALM. Deployment plans define how transports move across the system landscape and support different change management scenarios.

These plans may vary depending on organizational requirements and client-specific processes.

Common examples include:

  • Normal Change
  • Standard Change etc.

Create the required deployment plans based on your organization’s change management process. After creating the plans, assign the system groups that were configured in the earlier steps. This ensures that transports are deployed according to the defined system landscape and transport routes.

By configuring deployment plans properly, SAP Cloud ALM can effectively manage and control the movement of transports across development, testing, and production environments.

In the next step, assign the required roles for Change and Deployment Management in SAP Cloud ALM. These roles define the responsibilities of users involved in the change management process.

Typical roles include Project Lead, Change Manager, Developer, and other relevant stakeholders. Assigning these roles ensures proper governance, clear accountability, and smooth execution of the change and transport management process.

With these steps completed, the configuration and integration with SAP Cloud ALM are successfully finalized. The on-premise systems are now connected, enabling centralized monitoring, transport management, and change and deployment management across the system landscape

  1. System Refresh Considerations

After connecting your ABAP system to SAP Cloud ALM, the system continuously sends monitoring data to the SAP Cloud ALM monitoring applications.

The monitoring data is associated with a specific ABAP system in the Landscape Management application based on the Landscape Management System (LMS) ID.

When a system refresh is performed—where an ABAP system is overwritten with a copy of another system—the original LMS ID mapping may become inconsistent. As a result, monitoring data may incorrectly be assigned to the source system of the copy rather than to the refreshed system.

To prevent this issue, it is recommended to save and restore the tables containing SAP Cloud ALM monitoring configuration during the refresh process.

Post-Copy Automation (PCA) Integration

The SAP Cloud ALM configuration is integrated with Post-Copy Automation (PCA) to simplify system copy and refresh scenarios.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following components are available:

  • ST-PI 7.40 SP27 or higher
  • Implementation of SAP Note 3501074 (System Copy: ST-PI CALM Configuration)

Reports for System Copy and Refresh

If Post-Copy Automation (PCA) is not used, SAP provides dedicated reports to support system copy and refresh scenarios related to SAP Cloud ALM configuration.

Report: /SDF/CALM_SYS_COPY

This report is used after performing a system copy to create a new system based on an already configured ABAP system.

The report deletes the existing SAP Cloud ALM configuration associated with the copied system to prevent incorrect system mapping.

After running this report, the connection to SAP Cloud ALM must be re-established using transaction:

/SDF/ALM_SETUP

To prepare a system refresh, you can execute this report. It creates a Transport of Copies (ToC) with the table content of the SAP Cloud ALM configuration. Export this request and save it. After the system refresh, you can import this transport request again.

entry.title: How SAP QA Testing Services Reduce Downtime in Large Enterprises
entry.content:
Understanding the True Cost and Causes of SAP Downtime

Downtime in SAP environments is rarely caused by a single defect. It is often the result of multiple gaps across validation layers, integration points, and release practices. Without structured SAP testing services, these gaps accumulate and surface during peak business operations.

One of the most significant contributing factors to failures is a lack of complete process validation, which generally only utilizes limited functional testing to validate enterprise workflows with standard case scenarios. Since enterprise workflows include exceptions, dependencies, and real-time data exchange with other systems/locations, if these workflows are not completely validated through comprehensive testing, they are likely to produce errors when used.

Inadequate regression coverage is another major contributor to SAP downtime. SAP systems frequently receive updates, patches, and configuration changes, and if these updates and changes are not properly tested through thorough SAP regression testing services, they may render otherwise stable processes unstable. Examples of SAP regression testing services not properly executed include manually executing regression tests, therefore. potentially missing business-critical regression tests leading to a greater risk of downtime after executing major framework releases.

Integration complexity further amplifies the problem. Modern SAP solutions communicate with external 3rd party solutions via multiple APIs or applications. When there are no SAP integration testing services in place, there is a higher probability of data inconsistencies and workflow disruptions. Also, problems caused by integration between modern SAP and older SAP applications are often difficult to discover in isolated testing environments, while causing a significant negative impact on production systems.

The lack of automation further compounds the problem. Due to the absence of SAP test automation services, SAP testing cycles are slow and inconsistent. Time constraints often force teams to skip critical validations, thus allowing defects to escape into production. Together, all the gaps explain why downtime persists despite significant investments in SAP infrastructure.

Core Components of SAP QA Testing That Prevent Downtime

Reducing downtime requires a layered testing approach where each component addresses a specific risk area. SAP testing services create a measurable impact by combining multiple validation techniques into a unified and structured framework, ensuring systems remain stable under real business conditions.

Functional Validation for Business Accuracy

SAP functional testing ensures that core business processes operate exactly as intended in real scenarios. It validates critical workflows such as order processing, billing, and inventory updates, while also covering exception handling and edge cases that are often overlooked. This structured validation reduces the risk of process failures during live operations and keeps business continuity intact.

Regression Stability Across Releases

Frequent system updates require continuous and reliable validation. SAP regression testing services ensure that existing functionalities remain stable and unaffected after every change or enhancement. With automated regression suites improving both coverage and execution speed, organizations can release updates with confidence while minimizing disruptions to ongoing operations.

Integration Assurance Across Systems

Enterprise systems depend heavily on seamless integration. SAP integration testing services validate end-to-end data flow between SAP and connected external systems, identifying inconsistencies in data formats, APIs, and workflows. This prevents failures caused by system dependencies and ensures that all interconnected processes function without disruption.

Automation for Speed and Consistency

Manual testing alone cannot keep up with the scale and speed of modern SAP environments. SAP test automation services enable faster, repeatable, and consistent test execution across large and complex test suites. This not only supports continuous testing in fast-paced release cycles but also improves overall testing efficiency and reliability.

Role of Advanced Testing Strategies in Downtime Reduction

A structured testing approach requires more than tools and execution. It depends on well-defined strategies that align testing with business risk and system complexity. SAP testing services extend into planning and optimization through targeted methodologies that directly influence system stability.

One critical aspect is risk-based testing. Instead of testing everything equally, teams prioritize scenarios that directly impact business continuity. This approach aligns with SAP test strategy services, where high-risk transactions receive deeper validation. It ensures that testing efforts are focused and effective rather than exhaustive but inefficient.

Another important strategy is early validation within the development lifecycle. By integrating SAP functional testing at early stages, defects are identified before they propagate into later phases. This reduces rework and prevents production failures that often arise from late-stage detection. Early validation also improves coordination between development and testing teams.

Continuous testing plays an equally important role. With the support of SAP regression testing services and SAP test automation services, validation becomes an ongoing activity instead of a release phase task. Automated pipelines ensure that every change is tested in near real time, reducing the risk of introducing instability into production environments.

Measurable Business Benefits of SAP Testing Services

A well-structured SAP testing approach does more than identifying defects. It strengthens system stability and drives consistent operational performance.

Faster and Safer Releases

Frequent updates demand stability. Regression testing ensures existing processes remain unaffected, while automation reduces execution time. The result is quicker releases without compromising validation depth.

Improved System Reliability

Reliable systems depend on consistent validation. Functional testing confirms transaction accuracy, and integration testing ensures smooth data exchange. Together, they reduce inconsistencies that often surface post go-live.

Optimized Resource Utilization

Manual testing limits scalability. Automation shifts the focus toward complex scenarios instead of repetitive tasks. This not only improves efficiency but also allows teams to use their time more strategically.

Better Decision Making

Testing is not just execution; it is insight generation. SAP testing highlights risk areas and system gaps, giving teams the clarity needed to plan releases, prioritize fixes, and avoid costly errors.

Best Practices for Implementing SAP QA Testing at Scale

Scaling SAP testing requires more than tools. It demands a disciplined approach that aligns testing efforts with business priorities and system complexity.

Establish a Risk-Based Testing Framework

Not all processes carry equal weight. Prioritizing high-impact workflows ensures critical business functions are tested thoroughly, reducing the chances of failure where it matters most.

Build Strong Regression Coverage

Every system change introduces risk. A well-defined regression strategy ensures stability is maintained across releases, while automation helps sustain consistency without increasing effort.

Strengthen Integration Validation

Modern SAP environments rely on interconnected systems. Validating APIs, data flows, and dependencies ensures seamless communication and prevents breakdowns caused by integration gaps.

Scale Automation Across Testing Layers

Automation should not be limited to basic checks. Expanding it across functional and integration layers improves speed, increases coverage, and brings consistency to large-scale testing efforts.

Collaborate with the Right Testing Partner

Expertise makes a difference. A capable SAP testing partner brings structured frameworks, domain knowledge, and proven practices, ensuring long-term stability and reducing operational risks.

Final Say

Downtime in large SAP systems should be regarded as a business risk that impacts revenue, compliance, and operational continuity rather than just a technical issue. Therefore, it's critical to take a systematic approach to this problem using SAP testing services to create proactive control mechanisms rather than traditional support functions.

entry.title: Re: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Custom Currency Code f...
entry.content: Thank you for your response, but I think there might be a misunderstanding. My question is not about using different rates for AR vs AP invoices. **The core issue is:** - Company Code 1000 (US): Uses USD, needs rate 145.00 for USD→JPY - Company Code 2000 (HK): Uses USD, needs rate 148.00 for USD→JPY Both are using the **same currency pair (USD→JPY)** but need **different rates per company code**. **Question:** Can we maintain **company code-specific exchange rates** in the standard exchange rate table? For example: - USD/JPY, Rate Type M, Company Code 1000 → 145.00 - USD/JPY, Rate Type M, Company Code 2000 → 148.00 Or is the exchange rate table purely based on currency pair + rate type, without company code differentiation? **If company code-specific rates are not possible, I'm considering these alternative approaches:** 1. **Use different currency types per company code** (e.g., Currency Type 30 for US, Currency Type 40 for HK, each with different USD→JPY rates) 2. **Use separate ledger groups** if exchange rates can be differentiated by ledger group 3. **Custom logic via BADi** to reference different rate tables based on company code (if extensibility allows) Could you please advise: - Whether company code-specific exchange rates are supported in the standard configuration? - If not, which of the above approaches (or any other standard solution) would be recommended? Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Re: Using CCM Accrual Engine in S/4HANA 2022 Before Start...
entry.content:
Yes you can start using the CCM Accrual Engine in S/4HANA 2022 even before you are ready to run the full settlement/payment process. The accrual engine is designed to calculate and post rebate accruals automatically based on condition contracts, independent of whether you later execute settlement.

Here are the key points for your scenario:

1) Accruals without Settlement
You can configure condition contracts and activate accrual calculation so that rebate accruals are posted automatically during billing. This avoids manual accrual postings.

2) Customer vs. Customer’s Customer
Technically, accruals are posted against the contract partner (your wholesale customer). If the rebate is intended for their downstream customer (retailer), you still accrue against the wholesaler’s account. When you eventually pay the rebate, you clear the accruals through settlement.

3) Clearing Accruals
Even if you don’t run settlement now, the accruals remain posted. Later, when you pay rebates (either via CCM settlement or manual FI postings), you clear those accruals.

4) P&L Reporting at Customer Level
Accruals are posted to rebate expense accounts with customer assignment (via profitability segment or account assignment in the condition contract). This allows you to report rebate accruals in your P&L by customer. If you need reporting at the indirect customer (retailer) level, you’ll need to model that in your condition contract (e.g., contract partner = wholesaler, but rebate recipient = retailer).

entry.title: Transfer of SAP Readiness Check Analyses from SAP for Me to SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
A new feature in the SAP Readiness Check dashboard allows transferring analyses that were initially created in the SAP Readiness Check cloud application on SAP for Me to a customer’s SAP Cloud ALM tenant. Within SAP Readiness Check on SAP Cloud ALM, customers can automatically integrate SAP Readiness Check findings into SAP Cloud ALM Project and Task Management. For details about the automatic integration of SAP Readiness check findings, refer to this blog: Integration of SAP Readiness Check results in SAP Cloud ALM Project and Task Management.

All modifications made in the SAP Readiness Check analysis in SAP for Me, including status, scope settings, and comments, are retained and transferred.

This feature is especially helpful for customers who started the SAP Readiness Check during the Discover phase of a project (as recommended by SAP, see the RISE with SAP Methodology graphic below) and later provisioned their SAP Cloud ALM tenant.


Customers participating in the SAP Customer Evolution Program or running a North Star Service or Planning Workshop to plan their transition usually also perform the SAP Readiness Check in SAP for Me, providing access to the results to the SAP experts, and can then transfer the analysis including all comments and modifications taken in the service to their SAP Cloud ALM tenant to continue in their isolated environment.

It is important to note that the transfer process automatically locks the analysis on SAP for Me from changes. However, the analysis remains available on SAP for Me in a read-only mode. Meanwhile, on SAP Cloud ALM, the analysis can be further maintained and updated.

To execute the transfer, click the cloud icon in the dashboard header. The user requires authorization ‘Manage SAP Readiness Check Analysis authorization’ (see 3310759 - Revised Authorization Concept for SAP Readiness Check - SAP for Me). Following the transfer, the analysis in SAP Cloud ALM will be accessible for all users of this SAP Cloud ALM tenant with the Readiness Check Analysis Administrator, Readiness Check Follow-Up Manager, and Readiness Check Analysis Viewer roles.

If customers have several SAP Cloud ALM tenants provisioned, they can choose the target tenant before starting the transfer process. Depending on multiple factors, including the size of the analysis, the transfer may take a few minutes. If needed, analyses can transfer to more than one SAP Cloud ALM tenant provisioned by the customer.

Once the transfer completes, a success message displays the date and time stamp of the analysis transfer, along with the information that moving forward, you can only make changes to the analysis in SAP Cloud ALM. Note: Changes made to the analysis on SAP Cloud ALM cannot be transferred back to the original analysis on SAP for Me.

The analysis status on SAP for Me changes from "Available" to "Transferred to Cloud ALM", both in the analysis list and in the analysis dashboard header.

Once the analysis is available in SAP Cloud ALM, the integration mode is automatically enabled, and you can select findings in the analysis for integration into SAP Cloud ALM Project and Task Management. You may either assign the findings to existing follow-ups or create new requirements or user stories in SAP Cloud ALM Project and Task Management.

As always, we are looking forward to your feedback!

Your SAP Readiness Check team

entry.title: Re: How to implement a 5HP reverse process (inter-company...
entry.content:
Hello @jesseho271

If 5W2 is already included in CBC, you cannot add 2TS. See KBA 3608818 for details.

There is no conflict between 2TS and 5HP that I am aware of. What conflicts is 5W2 versus 2TS.

So, in practice, my earlier recommendation to use 2TS for the reverse process is only valid if 2TS is available in your tenant. If 5W2 is already active, then that path is blocked and you need to check the alternative solution described in KBA 3608818.

On your second point “Does 2TS also support the 5W2 group assessment?”. There is an activation restriction on that. What is clear is that 5W2 prevents you from adding 2TS.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Intercompany Subcontracting Process & Stock Traceabil...
entry.content:
Intercompany subcontracting in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud is supported, but the process is more limited than in on‑premise/PCE. There is no standard way to post component receipts in the subcontractor plant (S2) with reference to a PO or delivery; instead, the recommended best practice is to use the subcontracting purchase order and delivery processes to ensure traceability. For information, Subcontracting is covered under scope item 1Z1 (Subcontracting in Procurement). Intercompany processes are covered under scope item 4AN (Intercompany Cost Allocations) and 1W2 (Intercompany Sales). There is no dedicated scope item for “intercompany subcontracting with component receipt reference.”

For traceability purpose, I would recommend the below Best Practice

1) Use delivery documents
Issue components from S1 with outbound delivery, so S2 posts inbound delivery GR. This creates document linkage.

2) Avoid manual GRs
Manual postings without reference should be discontinued to prevent reconciliation issues.

3) Custom extension
If strict traceability is required, you can build a lightweight extension (via SAP Build Process Automation or side‑by‑side app) to enforce GR postings with reference.

4) Inventory reconciliation
Regularly reconcile intercompany stock movements using standard reports (e.g., stock in transit, subcontracting stock).

entry.title: Joule A2A: Handeling Multi-Turn Conversations
entry.content:
In my first post on the Agent-to-Agent Integration with Joule, I showed how to set up a minimal viable example. Without context handling, your agent forgets everything after each turn—making follow-up questions or clarifications impossible. This post covers how to enable multi-turn conversations between Joule and your custom agent using the A2A protocol's context management features.

Scenario

The scenario is straightforward: we have a custom-built agent integrated with Joule. Joule is an assistant used by multiple users and capable of handling multiple conversations at the same time. If our agent is a static one—like the currency converter shown in my previous post—it's enough to handle each delegated task as an individual conversation without history. But for this blog we want to think of a scenario where the agent might need additional input from the user in multi-turn conversations. For this purpose we look at the contextId and taskId that the A2A protocol introduces, to sync the conversation context between Joule and our agent.

Understanding A2A Context Management

To support multi-turn interactions, the A2A protocol introduces contextId and taskId, which together allow agents to preserve conversational continuity and manage individual units of work across multiple requests.

Think of it this way:

  • contextId = the conversation. All messages between a user and agent that belong together share the same contextId. It's your memory key.
  • taskId = a specific job within that conversation. One conversation can have multiple tasks, each with its own lifecycle.
Example Flow
│
├── "Convert 300 USD to ?"
│     └── Task 1 (taskId: T1)
│           → input_required
│
├── "What is EUR rate?"
│     └── Task 2 (taskId: T2)
│           → completed

The agent creates both IDs. On the first request, neither exists—the agent generates them and returns them in the response. The client (Joule) then sends them back on subsequent requests to continue the conversation.

This separation allows clients to either continue a specific task, create a new task within an existing conversation, or manage multiple related tasks under one shared context—making A2A interactions stateful, structured, and scalable.

For multi-turn agents, tasks progress through defined lifecycle states

(working → input_required → completed)

Who manages these transitions? The agent's LLM does. When the model generates a response, it evaluates whether the user's input is sufficient to complete the task. If not, it signals input_required to request more information.

Find more details in the A2A Protocol documentation: https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/#34-multi-turn-interactions 


How Joule A2A Implements Context Management

Joule uses the agent-request action type to communicate with A2A agents. This action gives us control over what parameters are included in the request body.

By default, Joule only sends the current user message that triggered the agent request—not the entire chat history. This is an important detail: your agent won't automatically see previous conversation turns unless you explicitly manage context. While you could potentially append history using capability context, the standard approach is to let the agent maintain its own memory using the context ID.

In addition to the user message, we can include contextId and taskId in the request body. Since Joule acts as an A2A client, its primary responsibility is to propagate these IDs back to the agent on subsequent requests. This is achieved using capability context variables—session-scoped variables that persist across multiple interactions within a Joule conversation.

One convenient detail: you might expect to need conditional logic like "if contextId is null, omit the parameter." But Joule handles empty values gracefully, so no special handling is required on the first turn when the IDs don't exist yet.

Let's look at how this works in practice.

Joule Capability Configuration

The capability configuration implements the context flow described above. Here's the structure:

currency_agent_capability/
├── functions/
│   └── currency_agent_function.yaml
├── scenarios/
│   └── currency_agent_scenario.yaml
├── capability_context.yaml
└── capability.sapdas.yaml

An important point to understand: the agent supplies the IDs, not Joule. On the first request, Joule sends empty values. The agent generates new IDs and returns them in the response. Joule then captures these IDs and sends them back on subsequent requests.

Here's an example of the first request the agent — note that contextId and taskId are absent:

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "cc09e1b1-0a32-4160-9bcb-3336227b8c05",
  "method": "message/send",
  "params": {
    "message": {
      "role": "user",
      "parts": [{"text": "Convert 300 USD to", "kind": "text"}],
      "messageId": "cc09e1b1-0a32-4160-9bcb-3336227b8c05",
      "kind": "message"
    }
  }
}

The agent processes this request, generates new IDs, and returns a response with input-required status:

{
  "body": {
    "kind": "task",
    "contextId": "98fa8d22-0e99-4efe-a1a9-fff535e7db9d",
    "id": "f0d78896-0eab-472f-8a85-73050948c0a2",
    "history": [
      {
        "role": "user",
        "kind": "message",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "What is the exchange rate for USD?"}],
        "messageId": "eb8b5afa-63cb-4cc5-9b24-38f85e782c95"
      },
      {
        "role": "agent",
        "kind": "message",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "Looking up the exchange rates..."}]
      }
    ],
    "status": {
      "state": "input-required",
      "message": {
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "Which currency would you like to convert USD to?"}]
      }
    }
  }
}

Joule captures the contextId and taskId from this response. On the second request, both IDs are now included:

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "87cc08ae-8fc3-4d5c-a4ab-9dafd77d23a8",
  "method": "message/send",
  "params": {
    "message": {
      "role": "user",
      "parts": [{"text": "EUR", "kind": "text"}],
      "messageId": "87cc08ae-8fc3-4d5c-a4ab-9dafd77d23a8",
      "contextId": "98fa8d22-0e99-4efe-a1a9-fff535e7db9d",
      "taskId": "f0d78896-0eab-472f-8a85-73050948c0a2",
      "kind": "message"
    }
  }
}

In subsequent steps the ID's are passed along accordingly. Lets have a look how we can store them in Joule across messages:

Capability Context Definition

For that, we define variables in the capability context - to hold the context ID and task ID. These variables persist across multiple interactions within the same Joule session:

# capability_context.yaml
variables:
  - name: agent_context_id
  - name: agent_task_id


Scenario Configuration

The scenario defines which parameters flow in and which capability context variables are set with the result. In here we add the two variables:

# scenarios/currency_agent_scenario.yaml
description: The Currency Agent supports converting between different currencies.
target:
  name: currency_agent_function
  type: function
  parameters:
    - name: agent_context_id
      value: $capability_context.agent_context_id
    - name: agent_task_id
      value: $capability_context.agent_task_id

capability_context:
  - name: agent_context_id
    value: $target_result.agent_context_id
  - name: agent_task_id
    value: $target_result.agent_task_id


Function Definition

The function handles the actual A2A agent request and it now needs to send the ID's and ensure they are read from the response:

# functions/currency_agent_function.yaml
parameters:
  - name: agent_context_id
    optional: true
  - name: agent_task_id
    optional: true

action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: agent-request
        system_alias: CURRENCY_AGENT
        agent_type: remote
        body:
          contextId: "<? agent_context_id ?>"
          taskId: "<? agent_task_id ?>"
        result_variable: "_agent_response"
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "LOG: Context ID: <? _agent_response.body.contextId ?> | Task ID: <? _agent_response.body.id ?> | Status: <? _agent_response.body.status.state ?>"
  - condition: _agent_response.body != null && _agent_response.body.status != null && _agent_response.body.status.state == "input-required"
    actions:
    - type: message
      message:
        type: text
        content: <? _agent_response.body.status.message.parts[0].text ?>
        markdown: true
  - condition: _agent_response.body != null && _agent_response.body.artifacts != null && _agent_response.body.artifacts[0].parts != null
    actions:
    - type: message
      message:
        type: text
        content: <? _agent_response.body.artifacts[0].parts[0].text ?>
        markdown: true

result:
  agent_result: <? _agent_response ?>
  agent_context_id: "<? _agent_response != null && _agent_response.body != null && _agent_response.body.contextId != null ? _agent_response.body.contextId : '' ?>"
  agent_task_id: "<? _agent_response.body.status.state == 'completed' ? null : _agent_response.body.id ?>"

Key implementation details:

  1. We pass the variables into the agent-requests' body.
  2. Null-safe contextId extraction: The result uses a null-safe check to avoid errors.
  3. Task state handling: The result block sets agent_task_id to null when the status is completed, so the next request starts a fresh task. Otherwise, it preserves the task ID for continuation.
  4. Response routing: Two conditional action groups handle different response types—input-required shows the status message (the question to the user), while completed shows the artifact (the final result).

Note: This function implements extensive logic to manage taskId transitions. This level of handling is not required for all custom agents. If only contextId-based conversation tracking is needed, a simplified example is available on GitHub, while this version is retained here for explanatory purposes.

The Python Agent Implementation

Now let's look at how the agent code handles the context ID.

Agent Executor

The AgentExecutor is where context management happens. The A2A library handles most of the heavy lifting. Here's the core of the execute method:

# agent_executor.py
async def execute(self, context: RequestContext, event_queue: EventQueue) -> None:
    query = context.get_user_input()
    task = context.current_task

    # Create new task if none exists (first turn)
    if not task:
        task = new_task(context.message)
        await event_queue.enqueue_event(task)

    updater = TaskUpdater(event_queue, task.id, task.context_id)

    async for item in self.agent.stream(query, task.context_id):
        if item['require_user_input']:
            # Multi-turn: signal that we need more input
            await updater.update_status(
                TaskState.input_required,
                new_agent_text_message(item['content'], task.context_id, task.id),
                final=True,
            )
            break
        elif item['is_task_complete']:
            # Task complete: return the result as artifact
            await updater.add_artifact([Part(root=TextPart(text=item['content']))])
            await updater.complete()
            break

The key parts for context management:

  • new_task(context.message) — generates a new set of ID's if none exists, or reuses the one from the incoming request
  • task.context_id — the persistent ID that links all turns of the conversation
  • new_agent_text_message(content, task.context_id, task.id) — every response includes both IDs
  • TaskState.input_required — signals Joule that this is a multi-turn conversation

The Agent with Memory

The actual agent uses LangGraph's MemorySaver to persist conversation state. The key insight is using the A2A context_id as LangGraph's thread_id:

# agent.py
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent

memory = MemorySaver()

class CurrencyAgent:
    def __init__(self):
        self.graph = create_react_agent(
            self.model,
            tools=[get_exchange_rate],
            checkpointer=memory,  # Enables conversation persistence
            # ...
        )

    async def stream(self, query, context_id):
        inputs = {'messages': [('user', query)]}
        config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': context_id}}  # Links A2A context to LangGraph memory

        for item in self.graph.stream(inputs, config, stream_mode='values'):
            # Process streaming responses...
            yield self.process_message(item)

The critical connection is:

config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': context_id}}

By using the A2A context_id as LangGraph's thread_id, all messages in the same conversation share the same memory. This is how the agent "remembers" previous turns—LangGraph automatically loads and saves the conversation state based on this ID.

Note: In this example the task_id is not used on the Python side—I included it in the Joule capability to demonstrate the full pattern. For synchronous interactions where one task follows another sequentially, only the context_id is needed for memory. The task_id becomes relevant for async updates or when you need to reference a specific task later.

Result

With the above implementation, our Currency Agent can now ask for additional input and receive it within the same conversation context.

We see the consistent contextId and how the taskId is changing when a task is done.

With this setup, your agent can now ask clarifying questions, gather information incrementally, and maintain context across multiple exchanges—all while Joule handles the routing and user interface. If you missed the basic A2A integration setup, check out my first post on Agent-to-Agent Integration with Joule.

Note: What we have not yet seen is our agent being able to ask Joule the orchestrator or other named agents via this interface for additional input. While in pure pro-code we already are able to call other A2A capable agents, the Joule standard Agents are not yet consumable. Announced at TechEd 25 this will change soon.

Find the full code on Github. If ou have any questions write a comment!

entry.title: Re: FB03 Alternative in SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Best Practice for reviewing FI documents before SII submission

  • Use Manage Journal Entries or Journal Entry Analyzer Apps to extract the relevant purchase and sales documents.
  • Apply filters for posting date, document type, and tax codes to isolate the documents relevant for SII reporting.
  • Perform review in these apps before triggering the Spain SII Reporting process (scope item 1XI).
  • If needed, create a custom analytical report (via CDS view and Fiori app) that specifically lists the documents subject to SII, so reviewers have a single point of control.
  • Ensure workflow/approval is in place for journal entries, so corrections can be made before submission.

entry.title: Re: Any standard process for Oauth 2.0 refresh token setu...
entry.content:
Hello @Sushmitha_Nallaparaju1 

SAP CPI does not provide a standard Workday OAuth flow out of the box.
Configure OAuth 2.0 (Client Credentials / Refresh Token) manually in CPI:

Use OAuth2 Credentials in Security Material
Call Workday token endpoint to fetch access token


Store token and pass it in API calls via Authorization: Bearer token
Use Groovy script or Content Modifier to manage token refresh

This is the standard and supported approach for Workday–CPI integration.

 

Thanks,
Sravan Aleshwaram

entry.title: Default Price List Issue in Inventory Transfer

entry.title: Re: i want to create sales orders by using bapi using upl...
entry.content:
Hello @mohithvarma11 


ALV Upload Program - Sales Order Creation using BAPI


Data Declarations

TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_excel,
sales_org TYPE vkorg,
distr_chan TYPE vtweg,
division TYPE spart,
customer TYPE kunnr,
material TYPE matnr,
quantity TYPE kwmeng,
END OF ty_excel.

DATA: gt_excel TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_excel,
gs_excel TYPE ty_excel.


File Selection

PARAMETERS: p_file TYPE rlgrap-filename.

Upload Excel File to XSTRING

DATA: lv_xstring TYPE xstring,
lt_bin TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF solix.

CALL FUNCTION 'GUI_UPLOAD'
EXPORTING
filename = p_file
filetype = 'BIN'
TABLES
data_tab = lt_bin.

CALL FUNCTION 'SCMS_BINARY_TO_XSTRING'
TABLES
binary_tab = lt_bin
CHANGING
buffer = lv_xstring.


Read Excel Using `CL_FDT_XL_SPREADSHEET`

DATA: lo_excel TYPE REF TO cl_fdt_xl_spreadsheet,
lt_data TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF string,
lv_row TYPE i VALUE 2. "Skip header row

CREATE OBJECT lo_excel
EXPORTING
document_name = 'SO_UPLOAD'
xdocument = lv_xstring.

lo_excel->if_fdt_doc_spreadsheet~get_itab_from_worksheet(
EXPORTING
iv_worksheet_name = lo_excel->get_first_worksheet_name( )
IMPORTING
et_itab = lt_data ).

Map Excel Data to Internal Table

LOOP AT lt_data INTO DATA(lv_line) FROM lv_row.
SPLIT lv_line AT cl_abap_char_utilities=>horizontal_tab INTO
gs_excel-sales_org
gs_excel-distr_chan
gs_excel-division
gs_excel-customer
gs_excel-material
gs_excel-quantity.

APPEND gs_excel TO gt_excel.
CLEAR gs_excel.
ENDLOOP.

Adjust code accordingly.

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: 🇮🇳 Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding (Hyderabad, India)
entry.content:
This is the all-new SAP CodeJam for Joule Studio, a new tool in SAP Build that lets you create extensions to Joule so users can use SAP's digital assistant in their custom workflows.

The CodeJam is intended to introduce you to the Joule Studio tool so you can create your own:

  • Skills, which let you create predefined execution flows, triggered by a user's prompt inside the Joule chatbot
  • Agents, which lets you define complex scenarios that the agent can execute by iteratively reasoning and interacting with their environment to achieve a goal, using a selected LLM and triggered by a user's prompt inside the Joule chatbot

The session will include a short, simple introduction to the SAP AI tools and some basic concepts needed for working with Joule Studio—such as AI Core, document grounding, embeddings, and what is an LLM?. You will also have the opportunity to build agents directly using Python.

Prerequisites

  • Bring your own laptop with power adapter and have a supported Web browser of either Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge. Other browsers are not supported.
  • It can be helpful to bring a tablet or second laptop, if you have, to have the instructions on a separate screen.

Location and Date 

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Tuesday, June 23, 2026, from 09:30 - 16:00 local time in Hyderabad, India (IST). The language of the event and the content will be English.

Event Address: Tekskills India Pvt. Ltd, Survey # 1050, Plot # S - 2, 801 , 8th Floor, Manjeera Trinity, Corporate, JNTU Hi-tech City Road, KPHB 3rd Phase, Kukatpally, Hyderabad, Telangana 500072.

Google Maps: https://g.co/kgs/QHsuo1S

  • The event is kindly being hosted by Tekskills India Pvt Ltd. – THANK YOU!
  • The instructors are SAP Developer Advocates @Rekha_DR  and @shrinivasan_neelamegam 

Please note that spaces are limited! We can only accommodate 35 attendees. If you want to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. About 2 weeks before the event, you will be asked to confirm your attendance and will receive further instructions.

entry.title: Re: #CoE#TAIT What functionality is available in the SAP...
entry.content:
Dear @davidhe79,

The standard SAP Maintenance Assistant app allows technicians to handle parts/components at execution level before confirmation for example by reviewing required parts, issuing additional or unplanned parts and returning unused parts. SAP also documents that final confirmation can include clearing reservations. Based on SAP documented scope this is execution-side part handling, not full maintenance-order component maintenance

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/dfe7048eb27445619838125576edd247/2508/en-US/f8e902da2d6840ce83f2e07b2872f712.pdf

Summary:

In standard Public Cloud, technicians can adjust part consumption in Maintenance Assistant before confirmation, including adding unplanned parts and returning unused parts. Broader maintenance-order component maintenance is handled outside the mobile technician app

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: I copied a standard form with SBF transactioin, but t...
entry.content:
Hello @SAP-Slzr_1 


You copied only the Adobe Form , not the form interface (data source) .

Because of this, the copied form is not bound to the transaction data , so only static text prints.

Fix:

Copy the interface (SFP / form data interface) as well.
Assign the new interface to your new Z form .
Activate both interface and form .
Test again — transaction data will appear.

In short:
Form + Interface must be copied and linked together, not just the form.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: CX Onboarding: Organizational Change Management as the Key to a Successful CX Implementation
entry.content:
Organizational Change Management as the Key to a Successful CX Implementation

Customer Experience (CX) implementations create powerful opportunities to transform how organizations engage with their customers and empower their employees. When employees understand the purpose of the change, feel supported throughout the journey, and are equipped to work in new ways, CX initiatives become a catalyst for sustainable business impact.

In my previous blog, How to Prevent and Manage Project Risks Using SAP Best Practices, we briefly touched on Organizational Change Management (OCM) as a fundamental element in mitigating risk during CX implementations. In this article, we’ll take a closer look at what OCM really means in the context of CX, why it must be embedded from day one, and how clear roles and governance help ensure sustainable adoption.

What Is Organizational Change Management?

Cloud transformations fundamentally change how organizations interact with customers and, as a result, how employees work. New cloud systems influence daily routines, introduce new processes, redefine roles, and demand new skills, mindsets, and behaviours.

Organizational Change Management (OCM) ensures that people are prepared, supported, and enabled throughout this transformation. It goes far beyond communication and training. Effective OCM focuses on driving sustained behaviour change, helping organizations move past system setup and go-live toward long-term value realization and improved customer outcomes.

👉Check this blog to become familiar with OCM’s pillars: Launch Your Project: The Broader Perspective Digital Transformation.

Enabling OCM from the Start of the Project

In successful CX projects, OCM is not treated as an “add-on” or a last-minute activity. Instead, it is embedded into the CX initiative from the very beginning, starting in the Plan Your Implementation phase, to ensure alignment between strategy, technology, and people.

CX transformations are business transformations enabled by technology. They typically impact:

  • Sales, service, and marketing teams simultaneously
  • Cross-functional processes spanning multiple systems
  • Customer-facing behaviours and decision-making
  • Organizational culture, ownership, and accountability

When OCM is integrated into CX initiatives, organizations are better positioned to:

  • Drive faster and more sustainable adoption
  • Reduce resistance and change fatigue
  • Increase employee confidence and readiness
  • Deliver consistent, customer-centric experiences

Who Should Be Responsible for OCM in Your Project?

While it is essential to enable change management from the start, it is equally important—and a core pillar of the SAP OCM framework—to establish a clear governance model with well-defined roles and responsibilities. This includes having active executive sponsorship to lend credibility and reinforce the strategic importance of the project across the organization.

OCM is not owned by a single individual or team. Instead, it is a shared responsibility, supported by structured roles that may include:

  • Change Manager
    Owns the change strategy, plans and coordinates OCM activities, and ensures alignment with the overall CX program.
  • Project Manager
    Ensures effective integration between delivery and OCM activities, aligning milestones, communications, and training with the project delivery phases.
  • People Managers
    Play a critical role in reinforcing change locally, addressing concerns, and supporting their teams through the transition.
  • Change Network / Champions
    Act as ambassadors for the change, providing feedback from the frontline and supporting adoption within their areas.

Clear accountability across these roles helps ensure that change is actively managed and that adoption continues well beyond go-live.

👉Explore more about this topic with this learning: Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects.

How CX Onboarding Supports Customers with OCM

From the CX Onboarding perspective, we recognize how critical it is to establish effective change management early in the project lifecycle. To support our customers, we have compiled proven best practices into a simple, structured guide: CX Onboarding Guide for Organizational Change Management.

This content is designed to enable and support customer-led OCM efforts, not to replace them. It helps customers:

  • Understand what Organizational Change Management is
  • See how OCM aligns with the SAP Activate Methodology
  • Use a dedicated roadmap viewer with ready-to-use templates
  • Access learning and enablement programs to upskill change teams

We strongly encourage you to explore these resources and connect with us if you have questions. Our role is to provide guidance, tools, and insights so you can confidently embed OCM into your CX program.

Ultimately, the responsibility for ensuring that change management is a core component of the project, from initiation through post go-live, remains with the customer.

When OCM is treated as a strategic priority rather than a checkbox, CX implementations are far more likely to deliver lasting business and customer value.

 

Thank you for reading this blog. We hope you found the content insightful. Please give us a like or leave a comment; we’d love to hear from you!

💻Want to get in touch with us? Send an email to SAP_CX_Onboarding@sap.com.

📞You can also request a one‑on‑one call with one of our onboarding advisors using this form.

👉Key Resources

  • Onboarding Resource Center
  • CX Onboarding Guide for Organizational Change Management.
  • SAP Learning: Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects
  • Community: Launch Your Project: The Broader Perspective Digital Transformation
  • Community: How to Prevent and Manage Project Risks Using SAP Best Practices

entry.title: Re: Balance carryforward runs must be performed in sequen...
entry.content:
Hello @DL25 ,

What i understood from screenprints, Your system status is inconsistent between FI and Asset Accounting

-FI carryforward done only till 2023
-You are trying to jump to 2025
-Asset Accounting is already in 2026

 

Error / SituationExplanation & Fix
FINS_BCF_EN135 – Carryforward must be in sequenceYou are trying to run carryforward for 2025, but system has only completed up to 2023. SAP requires year-by-year processing.
FINS_BCF_EN041 – Run for consecutive yearsYou skipped 2024. System does not allow skipping years. You must first complete 2023 → 2024, then continue.
AA708 – Fiscal year change requested for pastAsset Accounting is already in 2026, while FI is behind. This is only a warning, not a blocking error.
Root CauseFI balance carryforward is incomplete, while Asset Accounting has already moved ahead.
Correct SequenceYou must run carryforward in order: ✔ 2023 → 2024 ✔ 2024 → 2025 ✔ 2025 → 2026 (if required)
What NOT to do❌ Do not skip years ❌ Do not try to go backwards ❌ Do not change AA fiscal year
Recommended ActionRun FAGLGVTR (or Fiori app) sequentially for missing years.
Important NoteCarryforward can be safely re-run multiple times without data issues.

If you find my response helpful, share your feedback. This motivates me to serve you better.
Appreciate your support.
Thanks,
Divyesh

entry.title: How are others using curricula effectively in the current learner experience?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

While the old homepage definitely had its issues, it worked better for our use of curricula than the current learner experience. We are finding that the new homepage makes it much harder for learners to use curricula the way we intended. Since the system has been moving in this direction for years and I have not seen much discussion about it, I wanted to ask if maybe I am missing something.

Our goal is for learners to complete a curriculum as one package, but the system, especially with the new homepage and other recent changes, seems to steer them toward launching individual items instead.

Two main issues we are seeing:

1) On the current homepage, individual items from their curricula are front and center under Required Learning, and there is no good way to force learners into the curriculum itself like we could on the old homepage. This often leads them to complete items out of order and/or to miss one or more items in a curriculum. For example, we do not want new learners just working through 100 separate items from the Required Learning section on their homepage - we would like them to go into the curriculum to complete all required curriculum items as a cohesive package.

2) Even if a learner opens the curriculum details page, after completing one item the completion confirmation pop-up takes them out of the flow. There is no easy way to return directly to the curriculum and move on to the next item. Instead, they are sent back to the homepage after each completion and have to manually navigate back into the same curriculum again. It has been like this for years - I believe that item completion pop-up was added in 2022 - and still nothing from support/influence to fix this; they say it is working as designed, but there is a breadcrumb link to return to the item's curriculum - you are just unable to press it after completing an item due to the completion pop-up.

Because of this, curricula do not seem very well supported from the learner perspective.

How are others handling this? Are you still using curricula as a learner-facing package, or have you had to adapt your approach?

entry.title: Re: YCOA Account Groups - Verification of Usage and Diffe...
entry.content:
Hello @byd ,

As per my understanding and experience:-

 

TopicABSTRECN
PurposeStandard reconciliation accountSpecial reconciliation account
Direct posting allowed Not allowed Allowed
Integration with subledger Mandatory Optional/Extended
Typical useNormal AR/AP postingsSpecial scenarios (adjustments, alternative recon)
RiskSafe, controlledMore flexible but risky if misused

Use ABST (Standard Case – 95% scenarios)

-Normal customer/vendor postings
-Standard AR/AP integration
-No manual FI postings required


Use RECN when you need:

-Direct FI postings on reconciliation accounts
-Alternative Reconciliation Accounts functionality
-Adjustments without subledger document
-Migration / correction scenarios

Appreciate your support.

Divyesh

entry.title: Re: Unlock the Potential of Characteristics-Based Planning (CBP) in SAP IBP
entry.content:
Hello, do we have any update regarding this one point....and if it is still restrictions what would be the workaround 

Data Integration:

CBP is supported by Real Time Integration. Out of the box, an end-to-end process is supported for S4HANA Private Cloud backend with Demand and Supply Segmentation. 

Please note: Real Time Integration is not supported for S4 HANA Public cloud. This restriction also applies to CHaracteristics Based Planning.

entry.title: Deep Dive: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland
entry.content:
Deep Dive: SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland

Die Ankündigung die Gehaltsabrechnung für den deutschen Öffentlichen Dienst (ÖD) in den Lösungsumfang der SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll aufzunehmen, hat eine sehr positive Resonanz bei ÖD Kunden und die DSAG hervorgerufen. Nun ist es höchste Zeit konkreter zu werden!

In diesem Blog möchten wir folgende Themen erläutern:

  1.             Was beinhaltet die Lösungsumfangserweiterung von SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP)
  2.             Ausblick für das Jahr 2026 und folgende

Was ist für ÖD Kunden eigentlich drin?

Hier empfehlen wir einen Blick auf die Länderspezifika für SF Employee Central Payroll - Öffentlicher Dienst Deutschland  zu werfen.

Zusammengefasst: der Lösungsumfang der SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (ECP) enthält ab 01. Januar 2026 alle Standardfunktionalitäten für die Personalabrechnung Öffentlicher Dienst Deutschland (PY-DE-PS). Die Fakten:

  • SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll basiert auf dem SAP HCM for SAP S/4HANA Stack
  • Die bekannten ÖD spezifischen Infotypen wie zB. IT0051 VBL/ZVE-Daten,  IT0509 Höherwertige Tätigkeiten,  IT0320 Dienstwohnung  IT0271 Statistiken des öffentlichen Diensts etc. sind wie gewohnt verfügbar
  • Pro Lohnart die im IT0014 Wiederkehrende Be-/Abzüge, IT0015 Ergänzende Zahlung oder IT2010 Entgeltbelege verarbeitet wird, wird definiert ob sie führend in  SF Employee Central oder in SF Employee Central Payroll gepflegt wird
  • Tarife wie zB. TVöD Bund, TVöD VKA, TVöD Pflege, TV-L etc. und ebenso die Beamtenbesoldung werden wie gewohnt abgebildet
  • ÖD spezifische Funktionalitäten wie zB. die automatische Berechnung der Urlaubs- und Krankenlohnaufschläge für unständige Bezüge, die Krankengeldzuschussberechnung, die zeitversetzte Verarbeitung der unständigen Bezüge oder die Altersteilzeit sind selbstverständlich im Standard vorhanden
  • Deutschland und ÖD spezifische Folgeaktivitäten sind im selben Umfang wie in SAP HCM for SAP S/4HANA bzw. bisher in SAP ERP HCM vorhanden
  • Das PY Schema D100 für die Abrechnung ist Teil der Auslieferung
  • Kundeneigenes Schemen und Zyklen können nach SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll übernommen werden
  • Customizing Aktivitäten und kundeneigene ABAP Programmierung z.B. über BadIs oder Z-Programme stehen zur Verfügung. Siehe Hierzu empfehlen wir die Dokumentation im SAP Help Portal zum Thema Modifikation und Erweiterbarkeit von SF Employee Central Payroll. 

 

Rahmenbedingungen für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland

Die wichtigste Botschaft: Der Scope der SF Employee Central Payroll  unterscheidet sich hinsichtlich der Personalabrechnung für den Öffentlichen Dienst Deutschland inklusive Folgeaktivitäten, Auswertungen und Statistiken nicht von SAP S/4HANA. 

Natürlich gilt der allgemeine Umfang der Lösung bzw. die Rahmenbedingungen gemäß der Produktbeschreibung und der Funktionsumfangsbeschreibung für SF Employee Central Payroll auch für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland. SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll ist der Teil der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite der für die Gehaltsabrechnung zuständig ist. Teile des HCM-Funktionsumfangs, die nicht direkt zur Gehaltsabrechnung gehören, sind wegen des starken Fokus auf Gehaltsabrechnung nicht im Lösungsumfang der EC Payroll verfügbar. Dazu gehören z.B. das Reisekostenmanagement, das Cross Application Time Sheet (CATS), die Learning Solution (LSO) oder eine Personaleinsatzplanung.

Dies vorausgeschickt möchten wir auf einige für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland wichtige Eigenheiten hinweisen. Zur besseren Lesbarkeit hier nur die zusätzlich für ÖD Kunden besonders relevanten Klarstellungen und Einschränkungen.

Kundenspezifische LösungVerwendung in Employee Central Payroll nicht erlaubt
Felder Dienstart/Unterdienstart in IT0001: Grundlegende Erweiterbarkeit: Zuordnung von Employee-Central-Feldern zu InfotypenVersorgungsadministration (VADM)
ZfA-Kommunikation über den SAP Standard hinausNachversicherungsadministration (NV)
 Organisationsmanagement Öffentliche Verwaltungen (PA-OM, PA-PM-OM)
 Stellenwirtschaft inklusive Obligoprozessor (PA-PM-CP) und Personal Budget Plan Management (PA-PM-BM)
 Reisekostenmanagement (FI-TV)
 Personaleinsatzplanung

Quelle: Länderspezifika für SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll - Öffentlicher Dienst Deutschland    Stand März 2026

Kunden aus dem Kern der öffentlichen Verwaltung z.B. aus Bund, Ländern und Kommunen, die VADM für die Versorgung Ihrer Beamten benötigen oder gesetzliche Anforderungen mit der Stellenwirtschaft befriedigen müssen, können dies nicht in der SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll (Stand März 2026). Weiteres dazu im Kapitel Ausblick.

Empfehlung für den Umgang mit Basisbezügen für den ÖD

Aufgrund der Verknüpfungen zu verschiedenen anderen ÖD-spezifischen Infotypen wird bis auf weiteres empfohlen, Basisbezüge (Infotyp 0008) im öffentlichen Dienst direkt in Employee Central Payroll zu verwalten und nicht aus dem Compensation Portlet in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central zu replizieren.

Dementsprechend sollten Basisbezüge (Infotyp 0008) von der Point-to-Point Replikation im Customizing (Transaktion SPRO) ausgeschlossen werden, indem Sie die beschriebenen Schritte aus der ECP Dokumentation vornehmen.

 

Voraussetzungen für SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll

Kunden die bestehendes Customizing und / oder kundeneigenen Code aus einem bestehenden SAP HCM System übernehmen möchten, können dies tun solange Sie:

  1. Funktionsumfangsbeschreibung, Produktbeschreibung und dabei insbesondere Modifikation und Erweiterbarkeit von SAP SF Employee Central Payroll beachten
  2. Sicherstellen, dass sowohl Customizing als auch kundeneigener Code mit SAP S/4HANA kompatibel sind. Beachten sie dabei insbesondere die SAP Note 3091160 - SAP HCM für SAP S/4HANA – Allgemeiner Ansatz und Strategie, welche Aufschluss über HCM Simplification Items in S/4HANA gibt

Als Startpunkt zur Identifikation von Simplification Items bietet sich der SAP Readiness Check for SAP SuccessFactors HCM an. 

 

Ausblick für das Jahr 2026 und folgende

Zusammen mit unseren Kunden begeben wir uns auf die Reise dem Öffentliche Dienst in Deutschland mit der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite moderne HCM Prozesse digital in der Cloud zur Verfügung zu stellen, ohne dabei Abstriche in Sachen Sicherheit, Souveränität und Funktionsumfang zu machen. 

SAP ist bewusst, dass besonders Kunden aus dem Kern der öffentlichen Verwaltung auf Funktionalitäten wie Versorgungsadministration oder Stellenbewirtschaftung nicht verzichten können. Zusammen mit unseren Partnern und der DSAG arbeiten wir an diesen und weiteren Themen um letztlich mit allen Kunden aus dem Öffentlichen Dienst das Ziel zu erreichen.

Es gilt der übliche Disclaimer: Dies ist natürlich keine Zusage bestimmte Funktionalitäten zu einem bestimmten Zeitpunkt oder überhaupt zu liefern. Die Roadmap wird sobald konkret absehbar über die dafür vorgesehen Kanäle wie Roadmap Explorer oder What's New Viewer kommuniziert.

HR souverän und sicher - SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite in der SAP Sovereign Cloud

Die Sicherheitsmaßnahmen beim Betrieb von SAP Cloud Lösungen entsprechen den höchsten Standards für Cybersicherheit, Betrieb und Datenschutz. Dies gilt insbesondere für kritische Persönliche Mitarbeiter- und Organisationsdaten in der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite. Ausführlich informiert hier die Webseite SAP Trust Center - Sicherheit.

Zusätzliche SAP-Sovereign-Cloud-Angebote, sind eigenständige Angebote unseres Cloud-Portfolios, die auf die anspruchsvollsten Anforderungen in allen vier Dimensionen der digitalen Souveränität zugeschnitten sind: Daten, Betrieb, Recht und Technik.
Damit werden die strengeren regulatorischen und sicherheitstechnischen Anforderungen bestimmter Branchen, wie in teilen des Öffentlichen Dienstes, gefordert erfüllt.

Besonders relevant für unsere ÖD Kunden in Deutschland ist das in Deutschland gehostete Angebot SAP Cloud Infrastructure. Die Verfügbarkeit der SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite inklusive Payroll ist dort bis Ende 2026 geplant.

Weitere Informationen finden Sie unter SAP Sovereign Cloud auf www.sap.com.

 

Fazit

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll basierend auf SAP S/4HANA  und tief integriert mit SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central  ist die moderne Payroll für den Öffentlichen Dienst in Deutschland. Dabei bietet sie aus Perspektive der Gehaltsabrechnung den vollen von SAP gewohnten Leistungsumfang in einem schlanken Kosteneffizienten Cloud Deployment. SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll vereint Effektivität, Effizienz und Sicherheit/Souveränität mit einer starken AI basierten Roadmap.

SAP arbeitet zusammen mit Partnern daran jedem Kunden den Umstieg auf die Full Cloud mit SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll zu ermöglichen.

Für Informationen zur SAP HCM Strategie, SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central, Readiness Check for SAP SuccessFactors und zu weiteren Themen, empfehlen wir einen Besuch unserer HCM Move Seite.

Has anyone encountered this? Anyone with data persistence knowledge can share anything more about it? Thank you!
